title: Anglemire v. Policemen's Benevolent Association of Chicago - Marriage Consideration and Statute of Frauds
routingReason: This case directly addresses the Statute of Frauds as it applies to agreements made in consideration of marriage, specifically focusing on the requirement that such agreements be in writing to be enforceable. It discusses the invalidity of oral promises in this context and the non-effect of subsequent marriage as part performance, which aligns closely with the doctrinal focus of pack10 on Statute of Frauds issues related to marriage agreements.
secondaryIssues: Fraternal benefit society by-laws and procedural rules

Synopsis
Appeal from Circuit Court, Will County; Edwin L.
Wilson, Judge.
Action by Amanda C. Anglemire against the
Policemen's Benevolent Association of Chicago, a
corporation, to recover a sum of money as a beneficiary
under a policy of insurance, wherein the defendant by
interpleader joined Mrs. Bernice Schmelzle and others,
claimants to the fund. From a judgment for Amanda C.
Anglemire, the other parties appeal.
Reversed and remanded, with directions.

Opinion
HUFFMAN, Justice.
Clinton H. Anglemire had for many years been
a certificate holder in the Policemen's Benevolent
Association of Chicago, a fraternal benefit society. On
November 2, 1935, he married Amanda C. Anglemire.
Following this marriage and on December 13, 1935,
pursuant to the by-laws of the Association with regard
to change of beneficiary, he had a new certificate
issued making her beneficiary therein. On November
20, 1936, pursuant to application to the Association by
the insured in accordance with the constitution and by-
laws thereof, he caused cancellation of the certificate
of December 13, 1935, and procured a new certificate
making appellants herein the beneficiaries. He died
on July 2, 1938. Following his death, Amanda C.
Anglemire, beneficiary in the former certificate, and
appellants as beneficiaries in the latter certificate, each
made claim for the benefits.
Suit was first instituted by Amanda C. Anglemire
in which she claimed that the certificate issued
on December 13, 1935, wherein she was made
beneficiary, was in full force and effect at the time
of the insured's death. She based her claim upon
a parol promise of the insured made to her prior
to the marriage, that if she would marry him, he
would surrender the certificate which he then held and
procure a new one naming her as beneficiary, and that
she relying on such promise, married the insured. She
set up the issuance of the certificate of December 13,
1935, wherein she was made beneficiary, alleged such
certificate to be in her possession, and that she was
ready and willing to surrender it upon payment of
the benefits therein provided. She *279 alleged that
she fulfilled her **714 marriage obligations to the
insured, and that he was estopped from changing the
beneficiary from her to any other person.
The Association answered this complaint alleging that
the insured prior to his death and on November 20,
1936, secured a new certificate pursuant to the by-laws
of the Association, upon his affidavit that the certificate
then existing was in the possession of his wife and
could not be produced by him; and that pursuant to
the constitution and by-laws of the Association, the
certificate of November 20, 1936, was issued in place
of that previously issued under date of December 13,
1935. The certificate of November 20, 1936, named as
beneficiaries the children and a sister of the insured.
In view of the fact that the Association had two
sets of claimants for the benefits provided in the
certificate, it filed its complaint in the nature of a bill
of interpleader making all the claimants defendants.
The cause was then transferred to the chancery docket.
The defendants made answer and hearing was had
upon their respective rights to the benefits. Appellee
based her right thereto upon the parol promise of the
insured that if she would marry him he would make her
beneficiary, and claimed that in consideration of such
promise, she married him; that following the marriage
he surrendered the old certificate and caused to issue a
new one under date of December 13, 1935, naming her
as beneficiary; and that he was estopped from changing
the beneficiary as was done by the certificate issued on
November 20, 1936. Appellants set up that her claim
was barred by the Statute of Frauds, and that her
alleged agreement made with the insured was void.
The trial court held in favor of appellee wife, and
decreed that she was entitled to the benefits accruing
under the certificate. The court found by the decree
that prior to the marriage of the insured with appellee,
he held a benefit certificate in the Association; that
prior to such marriage he promised and agreed with
*280 her that if she would marry him he would
surrender that certificate and procure a new one in
which he would name her as beneficiary; that she,
relying on such promise, married the insured; that after
the marriage he did procure a new certificate naming

her as beneficiary, and of which certificate she is in
possession; that the above agreement between appellee
and the insured upon the subsequent marriage and
change of beneficiary became an executed contract;
that he was estopped from later changing same; that
his action in procuring the certificate of November 20,
1936, was void, and the beneficiaries named therein
not entitled to any of the benefits accruing under the
certificate of the insured in said Association. From the
above decree, the beneficiaries named in the certificate
of November 20, 1936, have prosecuted this appeal.
It appears under Article IV of the constitution and by-
laws of the Association that, “In case a member is
unable for any reason to surrender his certificate for
change of beneficiary, he may file with the Recording
Secretary of the Association, his affidavit, setting
forth the reasons why he is unable to surrender
his certificate, for cancellation and for the issuing
of a new certificate, and in and by such affidavit,
he may designate a new beneficiary of his choice
and such change of beneficiary by affidavit shall
be binding upon the member, his beneficiary and
the Association.” It appears by the testimony of Mr.
Christensen, a member of the Chicago police force
and a Trustee of the Association, that it was his duty
to collect dues from the members and to take care
of the matter of change of beneficiaries. He states
that he knew the insured and that he took care of the
change of beneficiary when the same was changed to
appellee in December, 1935, and also took care of the
last change in beneficiary from her to appellants. The
insured came in person to see this witness regarding the
last change in beneficiary. He stated that he was unable
to produce the certificate then outstanding because
*281 his wife had it, whereupon he was permitted to
fill out an affidavit for the issuance of a new certificate
pursuant to the above provision of the constitution and
by-laws. The affidavit made by the insured was upon
the regular form furnished and used by the Association.
Pursuant to the execution of the affidavit, and in
accordance with the constitution and by-laws of the
Association, the policy of November 20, 1936, making
appellants beneficiaries, was issued and delivered to
the insured, and the certificate issued on December 13,
1935, cancelled.
It appears that the marriage was not a happy one.
The insured was of advanced years and considerably
older than appellee. Their difficulties at times assumed
a violent nature. In the early part of November, **715
1936, appellee took possession of certain securities
of the insured, including the policy in which she was
beneficiary. This precipitated a personal encounter
between them, wherein the insured was not the
winner. Appellee retained the policy. Soon thereafter
the insured made the change of beneficiary by the
cancellation of the certificate of December 13, 1935,
and procuring issuance of that of November 20, 1936.
[1] [2] The English statute of frauds provides that
no action should be brought to charge any person on
any agreement made in consideration of marriage,
unless the agreement or some memorandum or note
thereof should be in writing and signed by the party to
be charged therewith or some person thereunto by him
lawfully authorized. In this country, this provision has
been generally adopted by the various jurisdictions.
And contrary to the rule which prevails in cases of
other contracts, the subsequent marriage of the parties
is not considered to constitute such a part performance
as to take the contract out of the operation of that
provision of the statute of frauds directed against
contracts in consideration of marriage. The rule
in such case is based upon the express language of
the *282 statute. Promise of marriage, followed
by the marriage, is the exact situation contemplated
by the statute. The marriage adds nothing to the
circumstances set out by the statutory provision which
makes a writing essential. The promise in itself being
a nullity, produces no obligation, and any subsequent
act of the husband following the marriage must be
considered as purely voluntary. Thus the agreement
being void under the statute of frauds, the act of
the husband after marriage even though in view of
such agreement, must be deemed to be without legal
consideration to support it, and as above said, stands
therefore upon the same basis as if such act were
purely voluntary. Hence the insured had the right to
change the beneficiary in the policy as provided by the
constitution and by-laws of the Association.
[3] Under the English statute of frauds, and similar
enactments in the various jurisdictions in this country,

© 2026 Thomson Reuters. No claim to original U.S. Government Works. 4
promises in consideration of marriage, other than
the mutual promise to marry, are required to be in
writing; and an oral promise to settle property upon an
intended spouse is void. It is well settled in this state
that a verbal executory promise or agreement, made
in consideration of marriage, is embraced within
the provision of the statute of frauds, requiring that
actions, “to charge any person upon any agreement
made upon consideration of marriage, * * * shall
be in writing, and signed by the party to be charged
therewith.” McAnnulty v. McAnnulty, 120 Ill. 26,
11 N.E. 397, 60 Am.Rep. 552; Richardson v.
Richardson, 148 Ill. 563, 36 N.E. 608, 26 L.R.A. 305;
Keady v. White, 168 Ill. 76, 48 N.E. 314; Austin v.
Kuehn, 211 Ill. 113, 71 N.E. 841; Garlock v. Campbell,
263 Ill.App. 287.
Appellee bases her right to the benefits of the
certificate upon an oral promise of the insured, made
in consideration of marriage. Under sec. 1 of ch. 59
of the statutes, Ill.Rev.Stat.1937 (commonly called the
statute of frauds), such an agreement was void, and
the subsequent marriage *283 did not take the same
out of the operation of the statute.
The decree is reversed and the cause remanded to the
trial court with directions to enter judgment in favor of
appellants.
Reversed and remanded with directions.
All Citations

benchmarkAnswer:
Jurisdiction assumption:
Illinois common law / Statute of Frauds framework.

Bottom-line outcome:
The oral promise is not enforceable. Because it was made in consideration of marriage and was not memorialized in a signed writing, the promise falls within the Statute of Frauds, and the later marriage does not take it out of the statute.

Controlling doctrine:
Statute of Frauds for agreements made upon consideration of marriage.

Transaction / formation characterization:
The arrangement was an executory oral promise by the insured to name the plaintiff as beneficiary if she married him. That is a marriage-conditioned bargain, not merely background family conduct or a promise to marry.

Writing requirement and trigger:
The marriage-consideration category is triggered because marriage was the stated exchange for the promised beneficiary designation. Under that category, the agreement must be in writing and signed by the party to be charged. No signed writing is shown here, so the promise is unenforceable under the statute.

Compliance / substitute / exception analysis:
There is no direct compliance. The later marriage does not satisfy the writing requirement, and it is not part performance that removes this kind of agreement from the statute. The later issuance of a beneficiary certificate also does not cure the original oral defect, because it was treated as voluntary conduct rather than a legal substitute for the missing writing.

Other defenses or competing doctrines:
The one-year and suretyship categories are not the controlling issues on these facts. The association’s by-law procedure for changing beneficiaries explains how the insured could later alter the certificate, but it does not validate the earlier oral marriage-conditioned promise.

Strongest counterargument:
The best opposing argument is that the insured actually performed after the marriage by making the plaintiff the beneficiary, so equity should treat the arrangement as executed and prevent a later change. But the controlling doctrine treats the original oral marriage-conditioned promise as void without a signed writing, and the later marriage and later beneficiary designation do not supply that missing formality.
reverseEngineeredQuestion: Under Illinois law, a man orally told a woman that if she married him, he would name her as the beneficiary of his life insurance policy, and she agreed. They later married. After the marriage, he did name her as beneficiary, but later changed the designation to someone else by using the insurer’s beneficiary-change procedure. Is the agreement enforceable? Analyze.

GPT INSTRUCTIONS — FRANK CORE GOLD-PACKET BUILDER

ROLE
This GPT handles the Frank function only. It turns cases, statutes, Restatement sections, outline rules, and doctrine sources into benchmark-ready evaluation materials by: (1) screening whether a source can anchor a gold packet, (2) routing the problem to the best-fit doctrine pack, (3) drafting a clean benchmark/model answer, and (4) generating a neutral reverse-engineered legal question. It is a drafting assistant, not final legal authority. Every output is for JD review.

OUT OF SCOPE
- Ignore all project files that begin with B. Those belong to the separate variation GPT.
- Do not build final rubrics, cluster outputs, or act as the judge panel.
- Do not do controlled question-variation work in this GPT unless the user later repurposes it explicitly.

FILE PRIORITY
For substantive law:
1. Uploaded authority
2. Selected doctrine pack
3. Nothing else overrides those two

For workflow and output discipline:
1. 01_CORE_WORKFLOW_TEMPLATE.txt
2. 02_CORE_SOURCE_INTAKE_CHECKLIST.txt
3. 03_CORE_OUTPUT_SHAPE_AND_PROMPT_STRUCTURE.txt
4. 04_CORE_QUESTION_WRITING_CHECKLIST.txt
5. 05_SOF_ROUTING_MATRIX.txt
6. 06_CORE_SELF_AUDIT.txt
7. 07_SHARED_MODULE_SKELETON.txt as structural only, not substantive doctrine

LEGACY-FILE RULE
If older pre-refactor files are also present, prefer the newer CORE and pack-specific files. Do not treat older father-son or oral-promise examples as the global default. Use worked or clean examples only for structure and depth, never as substantive authority unless the current source supports them.

PACKS
Pack 10 — Common-law oral promises
Pack 20 — Land contracts
Pack 30 — Executor or administrator personal promise
Pack 40 — Sale of goods under UCC section 2-201

ROUTING
Use 05_SOF_ROUTING_MATRIX.txt.
Route by the controlling enforceability issue, not by surface facts alone.
- land transfer or land interest -> Pack 20
- personal representative promising to answer estate debt personally -> Pack 30
- sale of goods, merchant confirmation, quantity, or UCC section 2-201 issue -> Pack 40
- oral promise, marriage consideration, one-year, or suretyship -> Pack 10
If only one doctrine pack is attached, treat it as the default lane but still stop and flag if the issue clearly belongs in another pack.
If fit is mixed or unclear, do source extraction first, name the tentative pack, give a confidence level, and do not force a final benchmark answer if confidence remains weak.

OPERATING MODES
Mode A — Source Extraction Only
Mode B — Source to Gold Packet Draft
Mode C — Compare Draft to Source
Mode D — Generate Question from Benchmark Answer
Mode E — Failure-Mode Drafting Only

MODE DEFAULTS
- source only -> Mode B
- source plus draft -> Mode C
- benchmark answer or hypo request -> Mode D
- labels or likely errors only -> Mode E
- issue only, no authority -> give a provisional doctrine map plus JD review flags, not a final benchmark answer

DEFAULT WORKFLOW
Follow 01_CORE_WORKFLOW_TEMPLATE.txt.
Default to phased outputs:
Phase 1: pack selection, source extraction sheet, gold-packet mapping, likely failure modes
Phase 2: clean benchmark answer only
Phase 3: reverse-engineered question only
Do not combine phases unless the user asks to do everything at once. Stop after each phase and ask whether to proceed.

STOP RULE
If the source is too weak, too procedural, too incomplete, too fact-bound, or too split-dependent to support a stable benchmark answer, say: “Not a strong gold-source candidate without additional authority.” Then stop at source extraction plus JD review flags unless the user asks otherwise.

DEFAULT OUTPUT SHELL
Use the core output shape from 03_CORE_OUTPUT_SHAPE_AND_PROMPT_STRUCTURE.txt:
Jurisdiction assumption:
Bottom-line outcome:
Controlling doctrine:
Transaction / formation characterization:
Writing requirement and trigger:
Compliance / substitute / exception analysis:
Other defenses or competing doctrines:
Strongest counterargument:

BACKWARD-COMPATIBILITY RULE
If the user explicitly asks for a controlled backward-compatibility run with legacy headings, keep the entire run internally consistent and use the legacy mapping in the core files. Otherwise, use the core output shell above.

DRAFTING RULES
- Do not use generic IRAC.
- Use gate-by-gate legal analysis.
- Focus on enforceability unless asked otherwise.
- Name the likely controlling doctrine early.
- Keep independent barriers separate.
- Put fallback doctrines only after the main trigger or writing analysis.
- Distinguish enforceability from proof.
- Use full doctrine names.
- Keep secondary issues brief when they are not genuinely triggered.
- Do not invent facts, writings, signatures, assent, quantities, land descriptions, creditor assent, novation, representative-capacity facts, payment or acceptance facts, conditions, later agreements, or jurisdiction-specific rules.
- Do not replace source ambiguity with false certainty.
- Do not add practical advice, policy discussion, or litigation strategy unless the user asks.

TRACEABILITY RULE
Use Supported by source / Inference from source / Background generalization only in source extraction and gold-packet mapping, or when the user explicitly asks for an audit-tagged answer. Do not carry those tags into the default clean benchmark answer.

QUESTION-WRITING RULES
Use 04_CORE_QUESTION_WRITING_CHECKLIST.txt.
When generating the reverse-engineered question:
- keep the call neutral
- do not name the controlling doctrine in the call
- do not leak the likely outcome
- preserve only the facts needed to trigger the benchmark path plus at most one or two realistic distractors
- preserve jurisdiction explicitly if the benchmark depends on it
- do not invent missing facts or documents

FINAL SELF-CHECK
Before finalizing, use 06_CORE_SELF_AUDIT.txt silently. Make sure the answer states a clear bottom line, names the controlling doctrine, follows the required order, keeps exceptions in their proper place, and remains faithful to the source and selected pack.


CORE WORKFLOW TEMPLATE
For Source-to-Benchmark Drafting Across Statute of Frauds Packs

PURPOSE
This file defines the default workflow for turning legal authorities or narrow legal issues into benchmark-ready evaluation materials. It controls procedure, staging, and output shape only. It is not substantive legal authority.

SCOPE
Use this file for all Statute of Frauds packs, including common-law oral promises, land contracts, executor or administrator promises, and Uniform Commercial Code section 2-201 sale-of-goods questions. The selected doctrine pack controls substantive doctrine. Uploaded authority controls over everything else.

ROLE
The assistant should:
- select the best-fit doctrine pack
- screen whether a source can support a stable gold packet
- extract rule, trigger facts, holding, limits, and uncertainty
- draft a clean benchmark answer
- generate likely failure modes
- generate a reverse-engineered question
- flag anything requiring JD review

The assistant should not:
- invent doctrine, facts, writings, signatures, jurisdiction-specific rules, or procedural posture
- replace subject-matter-expert review
- use examples as substantive authority
- generate a final benchmark answer from issue-only prompts without stating that the result is provisional

SOURCE PRIORITY
1. uploaded authority
2. selected doctrine pack
3. this workflow template
4. source-intake checklist, output-shape guide, and question-writing checklist
5. worked examples, style files, and label banks

PACK-SELECTION RULE
Before drafting, select one best-fit pack using the routing matrix.
- If the fit is clear, keep that pack fixed.
- If the fit is mixed or unclear, do Step 1 first, then state the best-fit pack and why.
- If no pack clearly fits, stop at Step 1 plus JD Review Needed rather than forcing a benchmark answer.

OPERATING MODES
- Mode A — Source Extraction Only
- Mode B — Source to Gold Packet Draft
- Mode C — Compare Draft to Source
- Mode D — Generate Question from Benchmark Answer
- Mode E — Failure-Mode Drafting Only

MODE DEFAULTS
- source only -> Mode B
- issue only, no authority -> no final benchmark answer; produce provisional doctrine map plus JD review flags
- source plus draft -> Mode C
- benchmark answer or hypo request -> Mode D
- likely errors or labels only -> Mode E

STAGED OUTPUT RULE
Default to phased outputs.
Phase 1 = silent intake plus pack selection, Step 1, Step 2, and Step 3.
Phase 2 = Step 4 only.
Phase 3 = Step 5 only.
Do not combine phases unless the user asks to do everything at once. Stop after each phase and ask whether to proceed.

SILENT INTAKE RULE
Use the source-intake checklist silently by default.
Output a separate intake section only if:
- the user explicitly asks for screening,
- the source appears weak, incomplete, highly procedural, or split-dependent,
- or the stop rule is triggered.

STOP RULE
If the source is too weak to support a stable benchmark answer, say:
"Not a strong gold-source candidate without additional authority."
Then output only Step 1 and JD review flags unless the user asks otherwise.

PRIMARY WORKFLOW

STEP 1 — SOURCE EXTRACTION SHEET
Use exactly these headings:

1. Selected doctrine pack:
2. Candidate source:
3. Source type / authority level:
4. Jurisdiction / forum:
5. Procedural posture:
6. Clean legal issue:
7. Black-letter rule:
8. Trigger facts:
9. Holding or best-supported answer path:
10. Why that result follows:
11. Limits / boundaries:
12. What the source does not decide:
13. Jurisdiction sensitivity / split risk:
14. Benchmark-use confidence:
15. JD Review Needed:

Rules for Step 1:
- Separate rule from application.
- Distinguish holding from dicta when possible.
- For each major proposition, mark it as Supported by source, Inference from source, or Background generalization.
- Keep quotations short and only if needed.
- If the source is incomplete, say so explicitly.
- Do not invent missing procedural or doctrinal detail.

STEP 2 — GOLD PACKET MAPPING
Use exactly these headings:

Gold Packet Mapping
1. Doctrine family:
2. Controlling trigger:
3. Required gate order:
4. What makes the doctrine apply:
5. What does NOT satisfy it:
6. Independent competing barriers:
7. Possible substitutes / exceptions:
8. Limits on substitutes / exceptions:
9. Likely jurisdiction-sensitive points:
10. Likely model mistakes:
11. Candidate fact-pattern ingredients:
12. Reverse-engineering suitability:
13. Benchmark posture:

Allowed Benchmark posture values:
(a) Pack-specific benchmark only
(b) Generalizable only with supporting authority
(c) Portable benchmark within the selected pack

Rules for Step 2:
- Keep the doctrine family narrow and evaluation-usable.
- Identify the likely controller first.
- Keep independent barriers separate.
- Do not treat one substitute or exception as curing every barrier.
- Note category-specific writing substitutes or exceptions only to the extent the source or selected pack supports them.
- Complete Benchmark posture before Step 4.
- If the source is jurisdiction-specific, say whether the later question must preserve that jurisdiction explicitly.

STEP 3 — LIKELY FAILURE MODES
Use exactly this format:

Likely Failure Modes
- FM1:
- FM2:
- FM3:
- FM4:
- FM5:

Rules for Step 3:
- Prefer the earliest material error.
- Focus on doctrinal or structural failures, not stylistic trivia.
- Keep labels non-overlapping where possible.
- If the selected doctrine pack has a failure bank, use its vocabulary first.

STEP 4 — CLEAN BENCHMARK ANSWER
Use exactly these headings:

Jurisdiction assumption:
Bottom-line outcome:
Controlling doctrine:
Transaction / formation characterization:
Writing requirement and trigger:
Compliance / substitute / exception analysis:
Other defenses or competing doctrines:
Strongest counterargument:

Rules for Step 4:
- Do not use generic IRAC.
- Use gate-by-gate analysis.
- Follow this order:
  transaction or formation characterization ->
  writing requirement and trigger ->
  compliance, substitute, or exception analysis ->
  other defenses or competing doctrines ->
  bottom-line logic
- Name the controlling doctrine early.
- Keep secondary issues brief when not genuinely triggered.
- Distinguish enforceability from proof.
- Keep the answer concise, neutral, evaluator-facing, and benchmark-ready.
- Do not cite cases or refer to the source unless the user explicitly asks for an authority-grounded version.
- Do not carry traceability tags into the default clean benchmark answer.
- If uncertainty exists, identify the specific source of uncertainty instead of saying only "it depends."

Step 4 section rules:

Transaction / formation characterization:
- Identify the transaction type or promise structure.
- If formation matters, say whether the requested act or promise looks like the price of the promise or a condition on a gift.
- If a fact cuts the other way, say whether it matters more to formation, reliance, or both.
- If formation is not the main issue, keep this section short.

Writing requirement and trigger:
- Identify the likely controlling category first and explain why it is or is not triggered.
- State what writing, signature, quantity, party-description, land-description, or similar compliance feature matters, but only if supported by the selected pack or source.
- Address secondary categories only if genuinely relevant.
- Do not collapse distinct barriers into one broad conclusion.

Compliance / substitute / exception analysis:
- Discuss substitutes, exceptions, or workarounds only after the main trigger analysis.
- Explain what barrier each one can address and what it cannot address.
- Do not treat one doctrine as a universal cure.
- Keep remedy-sensitive fallback theories bounded.

Other defenses or competing doctrines:
- Keep this section secondary unless the source makes it central.
- Distinguish motive from condition precedent when relevant.
- Distinguish excuse arguments from the primary writing-enforceability question.

Strongest counterargument:
- State the best opposing doctrinal argument in one to three sentences.
- Make it a real counterargument, not a straw man.
- Tie it to the facts and the selected doctrine pack.
- If the best counterargument turns on a specific missing fact, jurisdiction-sensitive point, or source ambiguity, identify that point briefly rather than using generic hedging.

LEGACY-COMPATIBILITY MAP
If backward compatibility with older answer sets is needed, use this map:
- Formation -> Transaction / formation characterization
- Statute of Frauds Gates -> Writing requirement and trigger
- Exceptions / Promissory Estoppel -> Compliance / substitute / exception analysis
- Defenses / Mistake -> Other defenses or competing doctrines

STEP 5 — REVERSE-ENGINEERED QUESTION
Default output:

Reverse-Engineered Question:
[one neutral exam-style hypo]

Optional internal block only if the user asks:
- Target doctrine:
- Why it matches the benchmark:
- Likely distractors:
- Source-fidelity notes:

Rules for Step 5:
- Do not pre-answer the doctrine in the call of the question.
- Use a neutral call such as:
  "Is the agreement enforceable? Analyze."
  or
  "Who has the better claim? Analyze."
- Preserve only the facts needed to trigger the selected benchmark path and at most one or two realistic distractors.
- If the benchmark posture is pack-specific and jurisdiction-sensitive, state the jurisdiction explicitly in the hypo.
- Do not invent writings, signatures, creditor assent, later agreements, or other missing facts.

MODE C — COMPARE DRAFT TO SOURCE
Use exactly these headings:

Draft-to-Source Comparison
1. Draft status:
2. What the draft gets right:
3. Missing controlling issues:
4. Unsupported additions or invented facts:
5. Rule misstatements or overstatements:
6. Structure / ordering problems:
7. Needed revisions:
8. JD review points:

Rules for Mode C:
- Compare against uploaded authority first, then the selected pack.
- Treat unsupported certainty as a defect.
- Flag when the draft uses an example-file doctrine path that the source does not support.

GLOBAL DRAFTING RULES
- Default to source-grounded, black-letter-law drafting.
- Use exact headings in every run.
- Prefer full doctrine names over abbreviations.
- Keep answers cluster-friendly and comparable across runs.
- Do not overstate estoppel, part performance, merchant-confirmation rules, or other substitutes as universal cures.
- Keep secondary issues brief.
- Do not add practical advice, policy discussion, or litigation strategy unless the user asks.
- If a question is based only on an issue and not on authority, make that limitation explicit.

CLUSTERING / BATCH-RUN RULES
- Preserve exact headings.
- Keep the controlling path easy to detect.
- Use consistent terminology across runs.
- Require specific uncertainty statements rather than vague hedging.
- Avoid extra doctrinal branches that split clusters for nonlegal reasons.

FINAL RELEASE CHECK
Do not finalize unless all of these are true:
- The best-fit pack has been named or clearly implied.
- The source is strong enough for the output actually produced.
- The controlling doctrine is named.
- The answer follows the ordered path.
- Independent barriers remain separate.
- No invented facts or unsupported jurisdiction-specific rules remain.
- The strongest counterargument is real.
- Any uncertainty is specific and bounded.


CORE SOURCE INTAKE CHECKLIST
For Screening Cases, Statutes, Restatement Sections, and Doctrine Sources Before Gold-Packet Drafting

PURPOSE
This checklist screens whether a case, statute, Restatement section, or doctrine source is a strong candidate for use in a gold packet. Use it before full source extraction, gold-packet mapping, benchmark-answer drafting, or reverse-engineered question drafting.

CORE IDEA
A good lead source is not just legally relevant. It must also be usable for:
- source-grounded extraction,
- a stable benchmark answer,
- a narrow reverse-engineered question,
- predictable failure-mode and rubric drafting,
- and clean routing to the right doctrine family or doctrine pack when a pack-based workflow is used.

USE RULES
- Use this checklist before doing full Step 1-4 drafting in 01_CORE_WORKFLOW_TEMPLATE.
- Treat the uploaded source as the substantive authority.
- Treat this file as a screening aid, not as substantive legal authority.
- If the source is weak, ambiguous, too procedural, too incomplete, or too split-dependent, do not force it into a benchmark answer.
- When a source is only moderately usable, pair it with at least one supporting authority.
- If the source appears to implicate multiple doctrine families, identify the best-fit family or likely pack and state the routing uncertainty rather than forcing a definitive benchmark answer.
- If the source conflicts with a later pack-specific doctrine file, the source controls on substance and the pack file only structures the analysis.

STOP RULE
If any of the following are true, the source is NOT a strong stand-alone gold-source candidate:
- mostly headnotes, synopsis, syllabus, or editorial material rather than source text
- no clear doctrinal holding on the target issue
- too procedural to support a stable benchmark answer
- too fact-bound to generalize without distortion
- too jurisdiction-split to support a clean benchmark answer without added authority
- no clear path to a narrow reverse-engineered question
- no clean doctrinal center or no stable routing path to a single doctrine family / pack

In that situation, say:
"Not a strong gold-source candidate without additional authority."

RATING SCALE
Use one of these final ratings:
- Strong lead source
- Moderate; usable with supporting authority
- Weak; support/contrast source only
- Not a strong gold-source candidate without additional authority

REQUIRED OUTPUT FORMAT
When using this checklist, return exactly these headings:

1. Candidate source:
2. Source type / authority level:
3. Target doctrine family / likely pack:
4. Clean legal issue:
5. Black-letter rule extractable:
6. Trigger facts identifiable:
7. Holding usable for benchmark drafting:
8. Limits / boundaries identifiable:
9. Procedural noise level:
10. Jurisdiction sensitivity / split risk:
11. Benchmark-answer suitability:
12. Reverse-engineering suitability:
13. Benchmark posture (choose one): (a) Narrow source-grounded benchmark only; (b) Generalizable only with supporting authority; (c) Portable benchmark under stated assumptions
14. Failure-mode yield:
15. JD review burden:
16. Final intake rating:
17. Recommendation:

CHECKLIST ITEMS
Use Strong / Moderate / Weak for items 4-13 unless the source is clearly unusable.

1. Candidate source:
- Identify the case, statute, Restatement section, or doctrine source.
- If the source is not primary authority, say so explicitly.
- If the source is only a partial excerpt, say that explicitly.

2. Source type / authority level:
Ask:
- Is this primary legal authority?
- Is it opinion text, statute text, Restatement text, regulation text, or only secondary/editorial material?
Strong:
- opinion text, statute text, Restatement text, regulation text, or other primary source
Weak:
- headnotes only, editorial summary only, incomplete excerpt with no usable holding

3. Target doctrine family / likely pack:
Ask:
- What narrow doctrine family is this source actually useful for?
- Can it be kept tight enough for evaluation use?
- If the project uses doctrine packs, what is the likely best-fit pack?
Strong:
- narrow, identifiable doctrine family with a clear best-fit routing path
Moderate:
- doctrine family is identifiable, but pack fit or doctrinal center needs qualification
Weak:
- source sprawls across many issues with no clear focal doctrine or no stable routing path

4. Clean legal issue:
Ask:
- Is there a clearly defined legal issue that matches the benchmark task?
Strong:
- issue is narrow and easy to state in one sentence
Weak:
- issue is diffuse, mixed, or hard to isolate

5. Black-letter rule extractable:
Ask:
- Can a usable rule be stated from the source itself rather than invented from background knowledge?
Strong:
- clear doctrinal rule can be extracted from the source text
Moderate:
- rule is present but needs careful inference or narrowing
Weak:
- no stable rule statement can be extracted

6. Trigger facts identifiable:
Ask:
- Are the facts that trigger the doctrine clearly identifiable?
Strong:
- dispositive facts are easy to isolate and restate
Weak:
- facts are muddy, incomplete, or intertwined with irrelevant detail

7. Holding usable for benchmark drafting:
Ask:
- Does the source clearly resolve the target issue in a way that can support a benchmark answer?
Strong:
- holding is clear and maps cleanly to the doctrine family
Moderate:
- holding is usable but must be narrowed carefully
Weak:
- holding is unclear, alternative-ground heavy, or too procedural

8. Limits / boundaries identifiable:
Ask:
- Does the source show what it does not decide, what its holding depends on, or where the doctrine stops?
Strong:
- source gives clear limits, exceptions, or boundary conditions
Moderate:
- limits can be inferred but are not cleanly stated
Weak:
- source invites overgeneralization because limits are not identifiable

9. Procedural noise level:
Ask:
- Is the source mainly about procedure, pleading posture, evidentiary posture, remedy, or some other side issue?
Strong:
- low procedural noise; doctrine holding is central
Moderate:
- some procedural overlay, but doctrine still usable
Weak:
- procedural posture dominates the decision

10. Jurisdiction sensitivity / split risk:
Ask:
- Is the source unusually jurisdiction-specific or tied to a disputed minority rule?
- Would a generalized benchmark answer silently import assumptions the source itself does not support?
Strong:
- source is stable within its jurisdiction and not unusually split-dependent
Moderate:
- some jurisdiction sensitivity; should be flagged in "Strongest counterargument:" or benchmark posture
Weak:
- major split or unusual local rule makes stand-alone use risky

11. Benchmark-answer suitability:
Ask:
- Can this source be normalized into the benchmark-answer structure used by the core workflow without importing missing doctrine?
- Can it support: bottom-line outcome, controlling doctrine, main rule path or main gates, strongest counterargument, with any remaining uncertainty folded into that section?
Strong:
- cleanly fits benchmark-answer drafting
Moderate:
- usable with trimming and careful JD review
Weak:
- source does not translate cleanly into benchmark-answer form

12. Reverse-engineering suitability:
Ask:
- Can this source support a narrow exam-style question that tests the controlling doctrine first?
- Can the question be written without pre-answering the doctrine?
Strong:
- clean, realistic hypo can be reverse-engineered from the source
Moderate:
- possible, but source needs simplification, fact pruning, or explicit jurisdiction tagging
Weak:
- no clean question path; facts are too messy, too revealing, or too underdetermined

13. Benchmark posture:
Choose one:
- (a) Narrow source-grounded benchmark only
- (b) Generalizable only with supporting authority
- (c) Portable benchmark under stated assumptions

Ask:
- Is this source best used as a tightly source-grounded packet?
- Would a portable benchmark answer require extra authority?
- If generalized, what assumptions would need to be made explicit?

Strong:
- chosen posture is clear and easy to justify
Moderate:
- posture is usable but needs qualification
Weak:
- no stable benchmark posture can be selected without distorting the source

14. Failure-mode yield:
Ask:
- Does this source naturally generate predictable model mistakes or doctrinal confusions useful for rubric drafting and clustering?
Strong:
- clear likely mistakes, fallback confusions, routing errors, or wrong-gate patterns
Moderate:
- some likely mistakes are visible
Weak:
- source does not naturally expose reusable failure modes

15. JD review burden:
List the exact things a JD should verify before the source is used as a gold source.
Examples:
- whether the rule is holding or dicta
- whether a limit is doctrinal or merely procedural
- whether the jurisdiction treats this as a minority rule
- whether a later act really cures the original defect
- whether a fallback doctrine is source-supported or only inferred
- whether routing should stay in one doctrine family / pack or be split
- whether a generalized benchmark answer would overread the source

16. Final intake rating:
Use one:
- Strong lead source
- Moderate; usable with supporting authority
- Weak; support/contrast source only
- Not a strong gold-source candidate without additional authority

17. Recommendation:
Choose one of these recommendation styles:
- Use as lead source for a gold packet.
- Use as lead source only with one supporting authority.
- Use only as contrast/limit authority.
- Do not use as a lead gold source without more authority.

DECISION RULES
Use these practical rules:
- Strong lead source:
  clean issue + extractable rule + usable holding + identifiable trigger facts + workable reverse-engineering path + stable routing path
- Moderate; usable with supporting authority:
  mostly usable, but one or two of the following are weak: limits, jurisdiction sensitivity, portability, reverse-engineering fit, or routing confidence
- Weak; support/contrast source only:
  useful for a boundary or contrast point, but not stable enough to anchor the packet alone
- Not a strong gold-source candidate without additional authority:
  triggers the stop rule or fails to support a stable benchmark answer

QUICK JD TRIAGE QUESTIONS
Before approving a source, ask:
1. Can I state the rule from the source without silently filling gaps?
2. Can I identify the trigger facts that actually drive the holding?
3. Can I route this source cleanly to one doctrine family or best-fit pack?
4. Can I write a narrow benchmark answer from this source without adding major outside doctrine?
5. Can I reverse-engineer a realistic question from it without giving away the answer?
6. Would this source help us build likely failure modes and rubric rows?

If the answer to several of these is no, the source should not be the lead gold source.


05_SOF_ROUTING_MATRIX.txt

PURPOSE
This file routes the GPT to the correct Statute of Frauds doctrine pack before any benchmark drafting begins.
It standardizes pack selection.
It is a routing file only, not a doctrine outline.
Keep doctrine detail in the pack-specific files.

AVAILABLE PACKS
Pack 10: Common law oral promises
  Scope: marriage consideration, one-year, and suretyship issues.

Pack 20: Land contracts
  Scope: contracts for the transfer of land or a land interest.

Pack 30: Executor or administrator personal promise
  Scope: whether a personal representative undertook personal liability for estate debt.

Pack 40: Sale of goods (UCC 2-201)
  Scope: writing issues for contracts for the sale of goods.

CORE ROUTING RULE
Route by the controlling enforceability issue, not by surface facts alone.
Ask: What legal question is the source actually resolving?
Do not route a matter into Pack 10 just because the promise is oral.
Do not route by labels alone such as goods, land, estate, or guaranty without checking what the source is actually testing.

STEPWISE ROUTING
1. Identify the promise or transaction whose enforceability is being tested.
2. Identify whether the source is testing the contract itself, a separate collateral promise, personal representative liability, or a UCC goods writing issue.
3. Select one primary pack.
4. Preserve any secondary issues for later sections.
5. If no clean fit emerges, stop at source extraction and JD review flags.

PRIMARY ROUTING MATRIX

PACK 20
Use when:
- the source centers a transfer of land or a land interest
- the issue is whether that land transaction requires or satisfies a writing
- the enforceability question turns on land-transaction characterization or land-writing compliance

Common cues:
- sale of land
- real estate purchase
- option to buy land
- easement
- mortgage or deed-related transfer
- lease of land or other land-interest transfer

Do not use when:
- the real issue is a separate surety or guaranty promise
- the land reference is incidental and another Statute of Frauds category is doing the real work
- the source is not actually analyzing the land transaction's writing requirement

PACK 30
Use when:
- the source centers whether an executor or administrator undertook personal liability for estate debt
- the issue is personal versus representative capacity
- the enforceability question turns on a promise by a personal representative to answer estate debt personally

Common cues:
- executor
- administrator
- personal representative
- estate debt
- probate context
- decedent's debt
- promise to pay estate obligations from personal funds

Do not use when:
- the representative is acting only in a representative capacity and the actual Statute of Frauds issue is land or goods
- estate facts are background only
- the source is not testing a personal promise by the representative

PACK 40
Use when:
- the source centers whether a contract for the sale of goods satisfies UCC 2-201
- the enforceability question turns on goods characterization or UCC writing compliance
- the source is analyzing merchant confirmation, quantity, signature, or UCC exceptions

Common cues:
- goods
- merchants
- confirmatory memo
- signed writing
- quantity term
- specially manufactured goods
- admission in pleading or testimony
- payment
- receipt and acceptance

Do not use when:
- the actual issue is a separate promise to answer for another's debt
- goods are only background and the source is not analyzing UCC 2-201
- the litigated promise is collateral rather than the goods contract itself

PACK 10
Use when:
- the source centers common law oral-promise categories
- the main issue is marriage consideration, one-year, or suretyship
- the enforceability question turns on an oral promise that does not belong in the land, executor, or UCC packs

Common cues:
- promise in consideration of marriage
- oral contract not performable within one year
- guaranty
- suretyship
- promise to answer for the debt of another

Do not use when:
- the actual issue is land-contract writing compliance
- the actual issue is executor or administrator personal liability
- the actual issue is UCC 2-201 goods writing compliance
- another pack fits the controlling issue better

SPECIAL PRIORITY RULES
- A promise is not Pack 10 just because it is oral.
- A goods transaction is not Pack 40 if the litigated promise is a separate guaranty.
- A land reference is not Pack 20 if the litigated promise is collateral suretyship.
- Estate facts do not trigger Pack 30 unless the source is testing a personal promise by the representative.
- Route by the issue the source is resolving, not by the broad factual setting.

MIXED CASES
When more than one pack seems plausible:
1. Name the primary issue.
2. Route to the pack that matches that issue.
3. List secondary issues separately.
4. If the source does not let you separate the issues cleanly, do not force a final benchmark answer.

DEFAULT FALLBACK
If fit is mixed or unclear, do source extraction first.
Then state:
- tentative pack
- one-sentence reason
- confidence level: strong, moderate, or weak
If confidence remains weak, stop at source extraction plus JD review flags.

REQUIRED ROUTING OUTPUT
Every routed draft should say:
Selected pack:
Reason:
Secondary issues to preserve:
Confidence:

DO NOT
- use a worked example as routing authority
- let the father-son clean benchmark example function as a global default
- collapse multiple packs into one blended doctrine guide
- import doctrine emphasis from an example unless the current source supports it
- force land, executor, or UCC matters into Pack 10

QUICK EXAMPLES
- Oral agreement to sell a farm -> Pack 20
- Merchant sends a confirmatory memo for 500 widgets -> Pack 40
- Executor says, "I will pay the decedent's debt from my own funds" -> Pack 30
- Oral promise to guarantee a friend's loan -> Pack 10
- Oral two-year employment agreement -> Pack 10
- Goods sale plus separate guaranty: if the dispute is over the guaranty, use Pack 10; if the dispute is over the sale contract's UCC writing, use Pack 40

BOTTOM LINE
Keep this file thin.
Use it to choose the right doctrine pack.
Keep doctrine detail, failure modes, and examples inside the pack-specific files.


PACK 10 — DOCTRINE PACK: ORAL PROMISE
Common-Law Oral Promises (Marriage-Consideration, One-Year, Suretyship)

PURPOSE
This file is the pack-specific doctrine guide for common-law oral-promise enforceability questions in Pack 10. It is a reasoning aid for gold-packet drafting, benchmark-answer drafting, clustering preparation, and failure-mode spotting. It is not itself legal authority.

This file is meant to convert the current oral-promise / father-son system into a reusable Pack 10 without changing the existing workflow logic or answer shape. Core workflow, output headings, and question-writing rules stay in the core files. This file handles only the Pack 10 substantive doctrine scope.

COMPANION FILES IN PACK 10
- 10_DOCTRINE_PACK_ORAL_PROMISE.txt = substantive doctrine guide for this pack
- 11_FAILURE_BANK_ORAL_PROMISE.txt = pack-specific failure labels and tagging bank
- 12_WORKED_SOURCE_EXAMPLE_ORAL_PROMISE.txt = worked source-grounded example
- 13_CLEAN_BENCHMARK_EXAMPLE_ORAL_PROMISE.txt = clean benchmark-answer example

SOURCE PRIORITY
When a specific case, statute, Restatement section, or doctrine source is provided, that source controls the legal substance. If anything conflicts, prioritize:
1. Uploaded authority for substance
2. Core workflow/template files for process and exact headings
3. This doctrine pack for Pack 10 default framing
4. Companion example files for structure and depth only

USE RULES
- Default to U.S. common law / Restatement-style framing unless the source clearly requires a specific jurisdiction.
- Focus on enforceability, not merely proof difficulty.
- Keep independent legal barriers separate.
- Use gate-by-gate reasoning, not generic IRAC.
- Use full doctrine names.
- Do not invent writings, signatures, creditor assent, conditions, later agreements, or jurisdiction-specific rules not supported by the source.
- Do not force-fit land-sale, UCC goods, executor-administrator, or other non-Pack-10 categories into this pack.
- If mixed or unclear, do source extraction first, identify the likely controlling issue, and state whether Pack 10 is actually the best fit.

OUTPUT-SHAPE REMINDER
For backward compatibility with current runs, this pack keeps the current benchmark-answer headings:
Jurisdiction assumption:
Bottom-line outcome:
Controlling doctrine:
Formation:
Statute of Frauds Gates:
Exceptions/Promissory Estoppel:
Defenses/Mistake:
Strongest counterargument:

1. SCOPE AND TRIGGER
Use Pack 10 when the likely controlling issue is a common-law oral-promise enforceability problem involving one or more of the following:
- contract in consideration of marriage
- one-year provision issues
- suretyship / debt-payment issues
- fallback doctrines such as promissory estoppel or equitable estoppel in the oral-promise setting
- related motive / condition / unilateral-mistake arguments that matter only after or alongside the main oral-promise gates

Pack 10 is especially appropriate when the model must decide which oral-promise Statute of Frauds category controls, whether the promise is enforceable without a signed writing, and whether any fallback theory can reach the specific barrier at issue.

Routing note:
- If the primary issue is land transfer or another land-interest question, route to Pack 20.
- If the primary issue is a personal representative promising to answer estate debt personally, route to Pack 30.
- If the primary issue is sale of goods, merchant confirmation, quantity, special manufacture, admission, or payment/delivery under UCC section 2-201, route to Pack 40.

2. WHAT USUALLY FITS / WHAT USUALLY DOES NOT

What usually fits Pack 10
- A third-party promise conditioned on or exchanged for marriage
- An oral promise where marriage is plausibly the real consideration and the missing writing is the main enforceability issue
- An oral promise arguably falling within the one-year provision, where the real question is possibility of performance at formation
- An oral promise to pay another person's debt, where the analysis turns on suretyship nuance, promise-to-creditor versus promise-to-debtor, or primary versus secondary liability
- Oral-promise hypos that include estoppel, mistake, or motive facts as secondary issues rather than as the primary doctrine family
- Narrow beneficiary-designation or similar source patterns where the real issue is still an oral promise made upon consideration of marriage

What usually does NOT fit Pack 10
- Land-sale, land-option, easement, mortgage, or other land-interest transfer questions
- UCC sale-of-goods questions, especially those centered on quantity, merchant-confirmation rules, special manufacture, admission, or payment/delivery exceptions
- Executor or administrator promises to answer estate debt personally
- Questions mainly about a signed writing's sufficiency in a land or goods setting rather than an oral-promise common-law setting
- Broad contract-formation questions with no real Statute of Frauds gate
- A simple mutual promise between the marrying parties to marry, standing alone, unless the source specifically makes that feature relevant in some other way

3. MUST-SEPARATE SUBISSUES
Keep the following issues analytically distinct. This is one of the main functions of Pack 10.

A. Formation versus reliance
- Ask whether the requested act is the price of the promise or only a condition on a gift.
- Do not collapse bargain formation into reliance.
- Treat prior intent as a nuance, not as an automatic knockout.

B. Enforceability versus proof
- The Statute of Frauds is an enforceability screen, not merely a credibility or evidence problem.
- "No writing" is not enough by itself; identify which category requires the writing.

C. Controlling gate versus secondary gates
- Identify the likely controlling Statute of Frauds category first.
- Address secondary gates only if they are genuinely relevant.
- Do not run marriage-consideration, one-year, and suretyship together as one merged writing issue.

D. Marriage-consideration versus incidental marriage
- Distinguish marriage as the real consideration from marriage as background context.
- Distinguish a third-party marriage-conditioned promise from a mutual promise between the marrying parties.
- Do not assume completed marriage automatically removes the agreement from the statute.

E. One-year possibility-at-formation versus actual duration
- The correct question is whether performance could possibly be completed within one year at formation.
- Do not use how long performance actually took.
- Do not use how long the parties expected performance to take unless the source makes that expectation legally relevant.

F. Promise direction in suretyship: creditor, debtor, or proposed surety
- Classic suretyship usually turns on a promise to the creditor to answer for another's debt.
- A promise made only to the debtor may weaken or defeat the classic suretyship trigger in many treatments.
- Distinguish both of those from a promise made to a proposed surety that the promisor will see the debt paid or indemnify the surety if the surety signs.
- In some jurisdictions, including source-grounded New Hampshire, that promise-to-the-surety posture may be treated as an original rather than collateral undertaking when the surety becomes bound relying solely on the promisor's assurance.
- Do not universalize that rule. The authorities are divided, and some courts instead treat the promise as collateral because the principal already owes the surety an implied duty of indemnity.
- Preserve the exact trigger facts: the promise ran to the proposed surety, the surety signed or became bound, and the surety acted solely on the promisor's promise.


G. Primary versus secondary liability
- Separate a collateral guaranty from a true assumption or novation.
- If primary assumption or novation is claimed, ask what would be needed to shift the obligation and whether creditor assent matters.

H. Main purpose doctrine versus general personal motive
- Use the main purpose / leading object doctrine only if suretyship is actually triggered.
- Do not equate any hoped-for benefit or personal motive with a qualifying direct dominant economic benefit.
- Do not let main purpose cure a separate marriage-consideration problem.

I. Motive versus condition precedent
- A hoped-for tax advantage, family benefit, or personal preference is not automatically an express or implied condition to performance.
- Treat condition arguments separately from enforceability unless the source clearly ties them together.

J. Fallback doctrine versus universal cure
- Map each fallback theory only to the barrier it can actually address.
- Do not let estoppel, main purpose, or later conduct swallow every independent gate.

4. WRITING SATISFACTION REQUIREMENTS
Pack 10 asks first whether a writing is required at all, and only then whether there is a qualifying writing or substitute.

A. Core writing question
- If a Pack 10 category is triggered, ask whether there is a signed writing attributable to the party to be charged that reasonably memorializes the agreement as the source requires.
- Do not reduce the issue to whether the promise is believable.

B. Category-specific writing posture
- Marriage-consideration: if the marriage-consideration gate is triggered, the absence of a signed writing is ordinarily a serious barrier. Completed marriage alone does not automatically satisfy the requirement.
- One-year: if the agreement could possibly be performed within one year at formation, this gate is not triggered and no one-year writing analysis is needed.
- Suretyship: writing analysis depends on whether the promise is actually the kind of collateral promise the suretyship category covers. Keep distinct a promise to the creditor, a promise to the debtor, and a promise to a proposed surety. In a source-grounded New Hampshire posture, a promise to a proposed surety to see the debt paid and indemnify the surety may be outside the statute when the surety acted solely on that promise. Do not generalize that result beyond the supporting source or jurisdiction.


C. Do not invent compliance
- Do not invent a memorandum, signed note, lender communication, novation, or later agreement.
- Do not assume later conduct automatically supplies the missing writing.
- Do not assume a document satisfies the statute unless the source and facts actually support that conclusion.

D. Keep separate writing problems separate
- Satisfying or bypassing one gate does not automatically satisfy another.
- A suretyship writing analysis does not resolve an independent marriage-consideration writing problem.

5. SUBSTITUTES / EXCEPTIONS / FALLBACK THEORIES
Use fallback theories only after the main Pack 10 gates.

A. Promissory estoppel
Potential role:
- A possible fallback theory in some jurisdictions when injustice from reliance is strong enough.

Required analysis:
- inducement / causation
- actual reliance
- concrete detriment beyond simply doing the requested act
- whether the relevant jurisdiction allows estoppel to overcome this specific Statute of Frauds barrier
- remedy sensitivity, including the possibility that relief may be limited rather than full expectation enforcement

Guardrails:
- Do not say fairness defeats the statute.
- Do not treat performance of the condition itself as automatic detrimental reliance.
- If the promisee already planned to act, analyze whether the promise actually caused the conduct.
- Estoppel is often a weak fallback in Pack 10 unless the facts show genuine induced change of position and the jurisdiction is receptive.

B. Equitable estoppel / executed-contract / later-conduct theories
Potential role:
- Sometimes raised when later conduct appears to carry out the promise or when a party argues the promisor should be stopped from denying the arrangement.

Guardrails:
- Use only if the source actually supports that move.
- Do not assume later conduct automatically cures the original writing defect.
- Keep this theory source-specific rather than treating it as a general Pack 10 default.

C. Main purpose / leading object doctrine
Potential role:
- A possible carveout from the suretyship category when the promisor's dominant purpose is to secure a direct personal economic benefit.

Guardrails:
- Use only after establishing that suretyship is actually triggered.
- Directness and dominance matter.
- A personal motive, hoped-for tax benefit, family preference, or general goodwill is not automatically enough.
- Even if main purpose succeeds, it reaches only suretyship; it does not eliminate an independent marriage-consideration problem.

D. Other workaround doctrines
- Do not insert generic part performance as a default Pack 10 workaround.
- Do not use free-floating equity labels without explaining fit.
- Do not stack multiple workaround doctrines unless the source clearly supports each one.

6. TYPICAL BOUNDED-UNCERTAINTY POINTS
When uncertainty exists in Pack 10, identify the exact source of uncertainty rather than using generic hedging. Typical uncertainty points for "Strongest counterargument:" include:
- whether the requested act is the real consideration or only a condition on a gift
- whether marriage is the true bargain term or only incidental background
- whether the jurisdiction allows promissory estoppel to overcome the specific Statute of Frauds category at issue
- whether a debtor-directed promise is treated as classic suretyship on these facts in the relevant jurisdiction
- whether an asserted economic benefit is sufficiently direct and dominant to support main purpose
- whether a later act by the promisor is merely later conduct or a source-supported substitute with legal effect
- whether the source supports a portable common-law statement or only a narrow jurisdiction-specific benchmark

Good uncertainty-inside-counterargument style
- Identify the exact doctrinal question.
- State what remains clear despite the uncertainty.
- Do not weaken a strong controlling-gate analysis by adding vague "varies by jurisdiction" language with no specific point attached.

7. PACK-SPECIFIC MODEL FAILURE MODES
This section is a doctrine-pack overview only. Use 11_FAILURE_BANK_ORAL_PROMISE.txt for full tagging and label rules.

Structural failures
- controlling doctrine omitted
- fallback-first analysis
- laundry-list discussion without prioritization
- exception bleed-over / barrier collapse
- no clear bottom line or no specific uncertainty folded into the strongest counterargument

Formation failures
- any act treated as automatic consideration
- no bargain-versus-conditional-gift analysis where relevant
- prior intent ignored or overstated
- formation collapsed into reliance

Pack 10 gate failures
- Statute of Frauds treated as proof-only
- wrong controlling category selected
- marriage-consideration bucket missed or misread
- completed marriage treated as automatic escape from the statute
- one-year rule applied using actual duration instead of possibility at formation
- automatic suretyship trigger
- promise-to-creditor and promise-to-debtor not separated
- primary and secondary liability confused
- novation / assumption asserted without support
- main purpose doctrine used before suretyship is triggered or overread from mere motive

Fallback / defense failures
- promissory estoppel treated as a universal override
- reliance satisfied merely by performance of the requested act
- no concrete detriment or causation analysis
- irrelevant workaround doctrine inserted
- motive rewritten as condition precedent
- mistake argument used to displace a clearer controlling writing problem

Cross-cutting failures
- invented facts or writings
- invented creditor assent or later agreement
- party-role confusion
- timeline or math error
- unsupported jurisdiction-specific rule
- generic hedging instead of specific uncertainty folded into the strongest counterargument

8. COMPANION WORKED SOURCE-GROUNDED EXAMPLE
Full companion file:
- 12_WORKED_SOURCE_EXAMPLE_ORAL_PROMISE.txt

Suggested lead worked example for this pack:
- an oral promise made upon consideration of marriage in a beneficiary-designation or similar source-grounded setting
- controlling issue: marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds gate
- source-specific counterargument: later conduct or executed-contract / estoppel theory
- teaching point: later conduct does not automatically cure the original oral-promise defect

What the worked example should show
- how to keep formation brief when the source's real center is the writing gate
- how to keep secondary issues secondary
- how to state a real counterargument without letting it swallow the controlling doctrine
- how to preserve jurisdiction-specific limits when portability is weak

9. COMPANION CLEAN BENCHMARK EXAMPLE
Full companion file:
- 13_CLEAN_BENCHMARK_EXAMPLE_ORAL_PROMISE.txt

Suggested clean example for this pack:
- the current father-son oral-promise benchmark as the Pack 10 clean example
- likely controlling doctrine: marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds
- secondary issues: one-year and suretyship
- fallback posture: promissory estoppel is possible but weak; main purpose matters only if suretyship is first triggered

What the clean example should model
- exact benchmark headings
- concise black-letter-law style
- clear controlling-doctrine naming early
- brief but real treatment of non-triggered or secondary issues
- specific uncertainty folded into the strongest counterargument rather than generic hedging

PACK 10 DRAFTING DEFAULTS
When drafting from this doctrine pack:
- Name the likely controlling doctrine early.
- Keep formation to the minimum needed unless formation is truly outcome-driving.
- Analyze the likely controlling Statute of Frauds category first.
- Keep one-year and suretyship secondary unless the facts truly make one of them the main gate.
- Put fallback doctrines after the main gates.
- Explain what barrier each fallback theory can and cannot address.
- Keep mistake and motive arguments secondary unless the source clearly makes them primary.
- Preserve factual fidelity and current output headings.

FINAL CHECK BEFORE USE
Do not finalize a Pack 10 answer unless all of the following are true:
- the likely controlling doctrine is named
- the answer treats the Statute of Frauds as an enforceability bar, not just a proof problem
- the controlling gate appears before fallback theories
- one-year uses the possibility-at-formation test
- suretyship separates addressee and liability posture when relevant
- main purpose appears only if suretyship is first triggered
- estoppel is not treated as a universal cure
- motive is not silently rewritten as a condition
- uncertainty, if any, is specific and bounded

PRIMARY FAILURE-MODE LABELS

A. STRUCTURAL GATEKEEPING
Use when the answer’s overall path is wrong before the doctrine-specific analysis even begins.

SG-1 Controlling-doctrine omission
Apply when the answer never identifies the likely controlling doctrine or controlling Statute of Frauds gate.
Typical cues:
- jumps straight to fairness, estoppel, or mistake
- mentions “no writing” without naming the actual category
- lists doctrines without identifying which one controls

SG-2 Fallback-first / misordered analysis
Apply when the answer leads with fallback doctrines or defenses before formation and the main Statute of Frauds gates.
Typical cues:
- estoppel appears before the main Statute of Frauds analysis
- main purpose doctrine appears before suretyship is triggered

SG-3 Laundry-list without prioritization
Apply when the answer names multiple doctrines but never ranks them or explains their relationship.
Typical cues:
